content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

March 8, 1977

Mr. Samuel V. McGrath
City Attorney, Richmond
Richmond, California 94804

Re: Economic Development Commission of the City of Richmond

Dear Mr. McGrath:

This is in response to your letter to Chairman Lowenstein dated January 13, 1977. The Commission will not issue a formal opinion on this matter because it does not present a substantial question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code §1832O(g)(2). However, I trust the following informal advice will enable you to resolve the matter.

Taking your questions together, the basic issue presented is whether or not the members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission (hereinafter the "EDC") are "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code §87100* and/or whether the Commission is an "agency" subject to the requirement that it adopt a conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code §87300, et seq.

Section 82048 defines "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency". The Commission has further defined "member" in §87000 by regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(1), which provides:

(1) "Member" shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions with decision-making authority. A board or commission possesses decision-making authority whenever:

*All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Samuel V. McGrath
March 8, 1977
Page Two


(A)  It may make a final governmental
decision;

(B)  It may compel a governmental deci-
sion; or it may prevent a governmental decision
either by reason of an exclusive power to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto
which may not be overridden; or

(C)  It makes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of time
have been, regularly approved without signifi-
cant amendment or modification by another pub-
lic official or governmental agency.


The powers and duties of the Economic Development Commission
are defined in §3.14.090 of the Municipal Code of the City of
Richmond, which states:

     3.14.090.  Powers and duties.  The specific
powers and duties of the commission are as follows:

     (A)  Assist city officials, develop, compile
and coordinate information regarding available
areas suitable for economic development in the
city of Richmond by reviewing staff work and
providing appropriate feedback;

     (b)  Upon request of the city council, review
proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use of
city-owned land and properties for economic develop-
ment in the city of Richmond by reviewing staff work
and providing appropriate feedback;

     (c)  Work with the port director on matters
related to the economic development of the port
of Richmond;

     (d)  Review appropriate zoning ordinances and
make recommendations to the planning director to
encourage proper zoning and orderly development of
areas suitable for economic development;

Samuel V. McGrath
March 8, 1977
Page Three


     (e) Work with state and local agencies,
governing bodies, public and private organiza-
tions as appropriate to encourage economic
development of the city.

     (f) Promote the interests of commercial
and industrial development in metropolitan
Richmond;

     (g) Perform other tasks related to the
city's economic development as requested by
the city manager or the city council.

     Based on these duties and powers, we cannot conclude
that the EDC is "decision-making" by virtue of possessing
any of the authority referred to in subsection (1)(A) or
(1)(B) of the cited regulation. There remains the question
whether they are the type of board or commission referred
to in subsection (1)(C)*. We conclude that as the EDC is
currently operating they are not. We are particularly
persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in
fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections
(b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. Furthermore, we have
reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-
year period. The tenor of those meetings can best be
described as an exchange of information and views between
certain city officials and the Commission members. Indeed,
the minutes tend to reflect frequent "briefing" of the
Commission by city officials and an information-gathering
type of questioning of these officials by members of the
EDC. Examples of this are the following meetings:

     July 14, 1976 - presentation by the Public Works
Director of the City and a representative of Stan-
dard Oil concerning oil spills and cleanup.
No votes taken.

     August 13, 1975 - presentation of the City Manager
concerning redevelopment events. Commission voted
to recommend establishment of an Economic Devel-
opment Department.

*In determining whether a board or commission is "decision-
making" within the meaning of subsection (1)(C) of the
regulation, we will analyze not just the apparent statu-
tory authority of the agency, but rather the practical
effects and consequences of its decisions or deliberations.

Samuel V. McGrath
March 8, 1977
Page Four


December 9, 1976 - report by a Police Captain on
security in downtown areas. No votes taken.

May 12, 1976 - report by the Director of Public Works
concerning the funding for and status of the Hoffman
Freeway.

February 11, 1976 - presentation by the Director of
Housing Department concerning statewide legislation
concerning the California Housing and Finance Agency;
its possible effects on the city; and what type of
housing could be built pursuant to the provisions of
state law. No votes taken.

The Economic Development Director of Richmond is present
at all meetings and frequently makes presentations and
answers questions concerning growth and development of
terminals and other port facilities.

We have similarly reviewed the minutes of the City
Council to determine whether the Council, or any other city
agency, has "over an extended period of time" approved the
Commissions's recommendations without significant amendment or modification, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700, supra.
There is no evidence that this is the practice of the
governmental agencies of the City of Richmond. Accordingly,
we do not think the members of the EDC are "members" of
a decision-making board. Hence, they are not "public
officials" within the meaning of §87100. Based on the facts
presented in your letters of January 13 and January 21,
it further appears that the members of the EDC fit within
the exception to the definition of "designated employee"
contained in §82019(c).

We therefore conclude, based on the facts presented, that
it would be a reasonable determination for the City Council
to decide that the EDC is not required to develop and submit
a Conflict of Interest Code. This advice is based on currently existing facts. A change in the powers, duties or
practices of the EDC would require reconsideration of this
advice.

Thank you for seeking this Commission's advice. If
we can be of any further assistance, please feel free to
call me at (916) 322-6444.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mfn

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

CITY OF RICHMOND

CALIFORNIA 94804

SAMUEL V. McGRATH                    (415) 232-1212, EXTENSION 351
CITY ATTORNEY
                                     January 13, 1977


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

     Please find enclosed herewith a copy of Chapter 3.14 of the
Municipal Code of the City of Richmond, California.  This Chapter
created the Economic Development Commission of this City in 1974
for the purpose of advising the "City Council and city officials
on proposed activities for development of the city's port and for
other overall economic development of the city."  (Section 3.14.020
of Code.)  The particular powers and duties of the Commission are
set forth in Section 3.14.090 of that Chapter.

     This is to request a written opinion on the following questions:

     1.  Is this City Commission a local government agency which
must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code
Section 87300?

     2.  If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative,
is each member of such Commission an individual whose position must
be designated in such Code in view of the definition of "designated
employee" in Government Code Section 82019(c) and in view of the
fact that the City considers the Commission to be one which serves
a solely advisory function?

     3.  Is each member of such Commission a "public official"
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 who must comply
with the requirements of that section?

     Would the answers to the above questions be the same if
Sections 3.14.020 and 3.14.090 of Chapter 3.14 were amended to read
as shown in the draft which I have attached to the enclosed copy of
such Chapter?

The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 13, 1977                                    Page 2


     I shall be happy to provide you with such additional information
as you may require in order to grant this request for a written
opinion.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [handwritten signature]
                                        Samuel V. McGrath
                                        City Attorney


SVMcG:ss
Enclosure

3.08.050–3.08.080     BOARDS AND COMMISSIONS

for the exercise of the powers conferred and the performance of the duties
hereby imposed upon it.

3.08.050 Special meetings. Special meetings of the committee may be
called in accordance with its bylaws.

3.08.060 Chairman, vice-chairman and historian. The committee shall
elect one of its members as chairman, one as vice-chairman and one as
historian. Said officers shall serve for a term of one year. The chairman, or in
his absence the vice-chairman, or in his absence a chairman pro tem elected
by the members present, shall call to order meetings of the committee and
shall act as the presiding officer thereof.

3.08.070 Meetings to be open to public – Records – Quorum. All the
meetings of the committee, whether regular or special, shall be open to the
public and the committee shall cause to be kept a proper record of its
proceedings. A majority of the committee shall constitute a quorum for the
transaction of business. Whenever a quorum shall attend any meeting of the
committee, the concurrence of a majority of the committee present at such
meeting shall be necessary to constitute an act of the committee.

3.08.080 Purposes and duties. The purposes and duties of the citizens
urban renewal advisory committee are to:
     (a) Serve in an advisory capacity to the city council on all matters
pertaining to the urban renewal program and any matters related to urban
development;
     (b) Stimulate, educate and promote an active interest and participation
by said council and the citizens of the city in community-wide development;
     (c) Enlist private as well as public resources for development of the
city in the best interests of its citizens; and
     (d) Perform any other duties referred to it by the city council.
(Source: Ordinance No. 1669.)


Chapter 3.14
ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT COMMISSION

Sections:
     3.14.010     Creation of commission.
     3.14.020     Commission purpose and operation.
     3.14.030     Appointment and qualifications of members.
     3.14.040     Terms of office of members
     3.14.050     Absence from meetings.
     3.14.060     Officers.
     3.14.070     Meetings.
     3.14.080     Rules and records.

(Richmond 10-1-75)     84

ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT COMMISSION                3.14.010-3.14.050

3.14.090    Powers and duties.

3.14.010    Creation of commission. There is created an economic
development commission.

3.14.020    Commission purpose and operation. The purpose of this
commission is to advise the city council and city officials on proposed
activities for development of the city's port and for other overall economic
development of the city. The commission shall work with the city manager
and the city planning director on projects which substantially affect overall
economic development in the city. The port director, or his representative
nominated by him, shall attend all regular and special meetings of the
commission and shall advise the commission on any matter before it, but
shall not vote on any such matter.

3.14.030    Appointment and qualifications of members. From September
16, 1974, inclusive, the commission shall consist of fifteen commissioners
who shall not be officers or employees of the city and who shall be
appointed by the mayor with the approval of the city council and shall serve
without compensation. Any vacancies on the commission shall be filled by
appointment by the mayor with the approval of the city council for the
unexpired portion of the term of the appointee's predecessor. Each
individual considered for appointment to the commission shall either reside
or work in the city and shall have a background which provides the
commission with expertise in a field related to economic development of the
city.
(Amended by Ord. 44-74 N.S.)

3.14.040    Terms of office of members. The term of each member of the
commission shall be for three years, provided however, that of the members
first appointed after the effective date of this section, five shall serve for a
term of one year, five shall serve for a term of two years, and five shall serve
for a term of three years. The number of years that the members first
appointed shall serve shall be determined by those members by lot at the
first meeting at which they are all assembled.
(Amended by Ord. 44-74 N.S.)

3.14.050    Absence from meetings. The absence of any member of the
commission from more than three regularly scheduled meetings of the
commission within any twelve-consecutive-month period shall constitute an
automatic resignation from the commission. Such a resignation shall not,
however, disqualify an individual from subsequently being appointed to the
same or any other city commission or board. The provisions of this section
shall operate prospectively so as to apply only to absences which occur after
December 30, 1974. Any and all absences of members occurring prior to that
date which did not result in resignations are excused. In the event of any

85                                (Richmond 10-15-75)

3.14.060–3.14.090 BOARDS AND COMMISSIONS

such resignation, the vacancy shall be filled by appointment for the
unexpired portion of term of the appointee's predecessor in the manner
prescribed in Section 3.14.030 hereof.
(Amended by Ordinance No. 56-74 N.S.)

3.14.060 Officers. The commission shall elect from among their own
number a chairman and vice-chairman who shall serve in such capacity for
one year. In the case of a vacancy in the position of chairman or
vice-chairman, the commission shall elect a successor who shall serve for the
balance of the predecessor's term. The commission shall appoint a secretary
who may be, but need not be, a member of said commission.

3.14.070 Meetings. The commission shall hold its first meeting within
thirty days after all of its members have been appointed. At such meeting,
the commission shall fix the time and place of regular meetings of the
commission which shall not be less frequent than once each month. All
meetings of the commission shall be open to the public.

3.14.080 Rules and records. The commission shall adopt rules for the
transaction of the business of the commission, which rules shall, among
other things, include the term of the secretary of the commission; the
manner of calling and giving notices of special meetings and hearings, and the
appointment and powers of standing committees. The commission shall keep
records which shall be open to the public of its resolutions, transactions,
motions, orders, findings and determinations.

3.14.090 Powers and duties. The specific powers and duties of the
commission are as follows:
(a) Assist city officials, develop, compile and coordinate information
regarding available areas suitable for economic development in the city of
Richmond by reviewing staff work and providing appropriate feedback;
(b) Upon request of the city council, review proposed agreements for
the lease, sale or use of city-owned land and properties for economic
development and make recommendations to the city council;
(c) Work with the port director on matters relating to the economic
development of the port of Richmond;
(d) Review appropriate zoning ordinances and make recommendations
to the planning director to encourage proper zoning and orderly
development of areas suitable for economic development;
(e) Work with state and local agencies, governing bodies, public and
private organizations as appropriate to encourage economic development of
the city;
(f) Promote the interests of commercial and industrial development in
metropolitan Richmond;
(g) Perform other tasks related to the city's economic development as
requested by the city manager or the city council.
(Source: Ordinance No. 25-74 N.S.)

(Richmond 10-15-75) 86

DRAFT

1/11-77

3.14.020 Commission purpose. The purpose of this commission

is solely to advise the city council and certain city officials

concerning the matters set forth in Section 3.14.090 pertaini;g to

the development of the city's port and the overall economic develop-

ment of this City. The Economic Development and Renewal Administrator

or a representative nominated by him, shall attend all regular and

special meetings of the commission.

3.14.090 Advisory duties.

(a) The commission shall advise the City Council,

City Manager, and Economic Development and Renewal Administrator

concerning the following matters:

(1) Available areas suitable for economic development

in the City of Richmond;

(2) Proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use

of City-owned land and properties for economic development;

(3) The economic development of the Port of Richmond;

(4) Proper zoning and orderly development of areas

suitable for economic development; and

(5) Promotion of the interests of commercial and

industrial development in metropolitan Richmond.

(b) The acts of the commission shall be solely advisory

and the commission shall not, therefore, have any authority to:

(i) Make any final governmental decision or compel

any governmental decision pertaining to any of the above matters or

to any other governmental matter; or

(ii) Prevent a governmental decision either by reason

of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a

veto which may not be overridden.

(c) Neither the City Council nor any of the above-named

officials shall be obligated whatsoever to approve any recommendation

which the commission may make in carrying out its duties. Such

Council and officials shall have full authority, in relation to

any commission recommendation, to disregard the recommendation or to

make significant amendments or modifications thereof.

sections.conclusion: We conclude that as the EDC is currently operating they are not. We are particularly persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections (b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. Furthermore, we have reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-year period. The tenor of those meetings can best be described as an exchange of information and views between certain city officials and the Commission members.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission are "public officials" under Government Code §87100 and whether the Commission must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code under §87300, considering that it functions in an advisory capacity.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that EDC members are not "public officials" requiring conflict of interest compliance because the Commission lacks decision-making authority; it functions primarily as an advisory and information-gathering body without final governmental decision-making power, veto authority, or regularly approved substantive recommendations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with some text quality issues on final pages. Document includes both the incoming request (January 13, 1977 letter from City Attorney) and the FPPC response (March 8, 1977). FPPC explicitly states it will not issue formal opinion under 2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18320(g)(2) but provides informal advice. Conclusion section reconstructed from body text as there is no dedicated conclusion section header.

opment Department. 
*In determining whether a board or commission is "decision-
making" within the meaning of subsection (I) 
(C) of the 
regulation, we will analyze not just the apparent statu-
tory authority of the agency, but rather the practical 
effects and consequences of its decisions or deliberations.

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY 
CITY OF RICHMOND 
CALIFORNIA 94804 
SAMUEL V. McGRATH 
(415) 232-1212, EXTENSION 351 
CITY ATTORNEY 
January 13, 1977 
The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
 
Dear Chairman Lowenstein: 
Please find enclosed herewith a copy of Chapter 3.14 of the 
Municipal Code oJ the City of Richmond, California. 
This Chapter 
created the Economic Development Commission of this City in 1974 
for the purpose of advising the !'City Council and city officials 
on proposed activities for development of the city's port and for 
other overall economic development of the city. If 
(Sect"ion 3 .14.020 
of Code.) 
The particular powers and duties of the Commission are 
set forth in Section 3.14.090 of that Chapter. 
This is to request a written opinion on the following questions: 
1. 
Is this City Commission a local government agency which 
must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code 
Section 87300? 
2. 
If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, 
is each member of such Commission an individual whose position must 
be designated in such Code in view of the definition of "designated 
employee" in Government Code Section 82019(c) and in view of the 
fact that the City considers the Commission to be one which serves 
a solely advisory function? 
3. 
Is each member of such Commission a "public official!! 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 who must comply 
with the requirements of that section? 
Would the answers to the above questions be the same if 
Sections 3.14.020 and 3.14.090 of Chapter 3.14 were amended to read 
as shown in the draft which I have attached to the enclosed copy of 
such Chapter? 


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
January 13, 1977  
I shall be happy to provide you with such additional information 
as you may require in order to grant th~s request for a written 
opinion. 
SVMcG:ss 
Enclosure

CONCLUSION: We conclude that as the EDC is currently operating they are not. We are particularly persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections (b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. Furthermore, we have reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-year period. The tenor of those meetings can best be described as an exchange of information and views between certain city officials and the Commission members.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest (916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 March 8, 1977 Mr. Samuel V. McGrath City Attorney, Richmond Richmond, California 94804 Re: Economic Development Commission of the City of Richmond Dear Mr. McGrath: This is in response to your letter to Chairman Lowenstein dated January 13, 1977. The Commission will not issue a formal opinion on this matter because it does not present a substantial question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code §1832O(g)(2). However, I trust the following informal advice will enable you to resolve the matter. Taking your questions together, the basic issue presented is whether or not the members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission (hereinafter the "EDC") are "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code §87100* and/or whether the Commission is an "agency" subject to the requirement that it adopt a conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code §87300, et seq. Section 82048 defines "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency". The Commission has further defined "member" in §87000 by regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(1), which provides: (1) "Member" shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions with decision-making authority. A board or commission possesses decision-making authority whenever: *All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Samuel V. McGrath March 8, 1977 Page Two (A) It may make a final governmental decision; (B) It may compel a governmental deci- sion; or it may prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or (C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without signifi- cant amendment or modification by another pub- lic official or governmental agency. The powers and duties of the Economic Development Commission are defined in §3.14.090 of the Municipal Code of the City of Richmond, which states: 3.14.090. Powers and duties. The specific powers and duties of the commission are as follows: (A) Assist city officials, develop, compile and coordinate information regarding available areas suitable for economic development in the city of Richmond by reviewing staff work and providing appropriate feedback; (b) Upon request of the city council, review proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use of city-owned land and properties for economic develop- ment in the city of Richmond by reviewing staff work and providing appropriate feedback; (c) Work with the port director on matters related to the economic development of the port of Richmond; (d) Review appropriate zoning ordinances and make recommendations to the planning director to encourage proper zoning and orderly development of areas suitable for economic development; Samuel V. McGrath March 8, 1977 Page Three
embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion but provides informal advice that members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission are not "public officials" under the Political Reform Act because the Commission lacks decision-making authority and functions primarily in an advisory capacity. The determination is based on analysis of the Commission's actual powers and the practical tenor of its operations over a two-year review period.
